title: Clinopodium cuneifolium
taxonomy: Clinopodium cuneifolium is classified as a flowering plant, belonging to the family Lamiaceae and the genus Clinopodium. It is a perennial herb native to the mountainous regions of East Asia. Clinopodium cuneifolium is often found growing in forests and grasslands, thriving in well-drained acidic soils. The plant's classification is further divided into the kingdom Plantae, phylum Angiosperms, and class Magnoliopsida. This classification reflects the plant's unique characteristics and evolutionary relationships.

intro: Clinopodium cuneifolium, also known as wedge-leaved Clinopodium or wild basil, is a perennial herb belonging to the mint family (Lamiaceae) and is native to the eastern United States. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antiseptic, and antiviral effects, making it useful for treating various health issues such as sore throats, respiratory infections, and skin irritations. The bioactive constituents of Clinopodium cuneifolium include volatile oils, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. Clinopodium cuneifolium is commonly prepared as teas, infusions, and tinctures, and is often used in herbal medicine to promote respiratory health and reduce inflammation.
